title: AI FOMO: Why You Feel It and What to Do About It
description: Stop trying to keep up with AI, let it keep up with itself for you.
contentMarkdown:
For most of human history, your output was capped by how many people you could talk to, how fast you could type, and how much information you could absorb in a day. Those were the real bottlenecks. Now, almost overnight, that ceiling has been lifted. The new constraint isn't bandwidth or access. It's ideas, both in quantity and quality.

That shift is disorienting, and it's the root of AI FOMO.

When the tools around you start compounding in capability every few months, keeping up feels like a full-time job. New "groundbreaking" releases drop constantly, and the volume of content is exploding because AI makes it cheaper than ever to produce. If you're on a subscription, there's a quiet guilt that comes with it: a nagging sense that you're leaving tokens on the table and that someone, somewhere, is getting more out of this than you are.

The deeper anxiety isn't really about tools, though. It's about relevance. The old playbook said master one skill deeply and you'll be fine. That's becoming less true. Value is starting to look more like a senior engineer or a good manager: someone who can see across disciplines, coordinate moving parts, and know enough about each domain to ask the right questions. Your specialized knowledge doesn't go away; it just gets most useful as the starting point for a productive agent loop rather than the thing you grind through manually every day.

Working smarter isn't a motivational poster anymore. It's genuinely becoming the differentiator.

## So What Do You Actually Do?

The most practical antidote is restructuring how you use AI, not just using it more. On the research and productivity side, the leverage is in agents and longer-running loops. A good autonomous workflow needs two things: a clear goal and the right tools. Set those up well and you're orchestrating rather than executing. The people who win in this environment won't necessarily be the ones who know the most. They'll be the ones who can build and direct the best loops.

On the content side, the instinct is to use AI to consume more. That's the wrong direction. Use it to filter. Give it rules, have it ask "is this noise?" on your behalf before anything hits your feed. The goal is fewer, higher-signal inputs, not a faster firehose.

## Why You Shouldn't Lose Sleep Over It

Here's the thing about the voices pressuring you to move faster: if AI is as transformative as they claim, then you won't be able to opt out of that change even if you tried. The transition will be broad enough that the question isn't whether you'll be part of it. It's just a matter of when and how.

Getting intentional now gives you a head start. But missing a product launch or skipping a trending paper isn't going to strand you. Take a breath. Build something useful. The goal is leverage, not anxiety.
